lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contract manufacturers, private-label producers, and co-packers that repeatedly formulate, blend, fill, package, or otherwise manufacture cosmetics and toilet preparations for external brand customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Brand owners that manufacture only for their own account, marketing-only brands, distributors, retailers, captive facilities, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B manufacturing revenue from formulations, batches, filling, packaging, testing, and related compliance support, commonly priced per unit, batch, or program and governed by specifications, minimums, and change orders.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to contract/private-label manufacturers and co-packers because branded consumer-product manufacturing and outsourced B2B production have materially different customer acquisition, pricing, retention, and transfer economics within the same NAICS code.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for the broader 3255/3256 group, while O*NET reports tasks for mixing/blending and packaging occupations across industries. I map recordkeeping, line monitoring, production scheduling, quality-data review, formula and label checks, customer service, and administration to exposed work, while physical loading, cleaning, sampling, repairs, and hands-on operation remain largely unexposed. Broader occupational data may misstate the contract-cosmetics mix in either direction.
a.rationale: Contract cosmetics plants combine repetitive production and packaging with document-heavy customer, quality, formulation, and regulatory workflows. AI can substitute more office and review work than plant-floor handling, but packaging and blending remain equipment- and labor-dependent.
a.change_evidence: Wage-weighted task-time studies across at least 20 LMM contract cosmetics plants, separated into formulation, compounding, filling, packaging, quality, regulatory, planning, customer service, sales, and administration.
caveats: No six-digit occupation table or contract-manufacturer-only workforce study was found.
Exposure does not assume autonomous batch release or removal of required quality accountability.

rho.bridge: Census measures AI use across all US businesses and NIST describes manufacturing-wide uses and barriers, not implementation of exposed work in contract cosmetics. I translate current diffusion and the availability of document, forecasting, vision, and maintenance use cases into a five-year implementation share; national and larger-firm rates likely overstate readiness at LMM plants, while the document intensity of MoCRA may make some workflows easier than the manufacturing average.
rho.rationale: Customer documents, formula comparison, label review, forecasts, deviations, and maintenance signals are feasible copiloting targets. Legacy systems, fragmented batch data, customer validation, cybersecurity, product-safety risk, and human release authority constrain implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Digital-maturity audits plus validated pre/post labor-hour and error-rate data from contract cosmetics deployments spanning ERP, eQMS, LIMS, vision inspection, planning, and MoCRA submissions.
caveats: AI use in any business function is broader than operational substitution.
Published manufacturing examples are not representative causal estimates.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition includes all establishments preparing and packaging toilet preparations, mixing outsourced producers with own-brand manufacturers. A substantial but uncertain share of the injected LMM-band population should be contract manufacturers or co-packers with external repeat programs and transferable operations.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm 325620 roster classified by contract-manufacturing share of revenue, ownership, standalone operations, EBITDA, and customer concentration.
caveats: Public establishment classifications do not distinguish brand owners from contract manufacturers.
Some hybrid firms have both owned brands and external programs, making eligibility threshold-sensitive.

s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability around one quarter is plausible for private LMM contract manufacturers given succession and strategic consolidation, but public business-dynamics data do not identify qualifying transfers or the narrowed lens.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and industry advisors, reconciled to the eligible firm roster and excluding minority, captive, and internal reorganizations.
caveats: No direct six-digit control-transfer rate was found.
High-growth beauty acquisitions often target brands rather than the contract manufacturer in this lens.

q.rationale: Per-unit and program pricing lets a manufacturer retain initial savings, but sophisticated brands rebid, seek productivity concessions, and compare co-packers. Qualification effort, formulas, packaging-line fit, service levels, minimums, and launch reliability create switching costs that preserve part of the benefit.
q.change_evidence: Representative MSAs, pricing schedules, cost-plus/open-book clauses, annual productivity commitments, rebid cadence, historical price concessions, and customer churn after operational improvements.
caveats: No representative contract set was available.
Customer concentration and volatile components or packaging can overwhelm the effect of AI savings.

d5.bridge: BEA/FRED measures US real consumption of personal care products, not outsourced manufacturing volume or the full 325620 basket. I use the observed increase from 180.942 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 188.392 billion in 2025 as a demand anchor, then moderate it because imports, brand insourcing, inventory, and mix can separate consumer demand from domestic contract production; this proxy may overstate domestic outsourced growth.
d5.rationale: Personal care is a repeat-consumption category with recent real growth. Product proliferation and compliance can support outsourcing, while imports, brand failures, channel volatility, and retailer bargaining limit confidence.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price customer shipments and order volumes from a panel of contract manufacturers, segmented by skin care, hair care, fragrance, color cosmetics, sun care, and other categories, plus customer insourcing and import shares.
caveats: Personal care PCE includes retail products beyond domestic 325620 production.
One year of recent growth is not a five-year forecast.

o.rationale: Physical formulation, blending, filling, packaging, testing, traceability, and facility responsibility remain necessary even as software improves design and administration. Displacement would mainly come from brands insourcing, importing finished goods, or consolidating programs with larger manufacturers.
o.change_evidence: Brand procurement interviews quantifying planned insourcing, finished-goods imports, vendor consolidation, and use of virtual or software-only product-development services without domestic contract production.
caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from production tasks and regulation rather than measured directly.
The responsible person under MoCRA may be a brand or distributor, not necessarily the contract manufacturer.

narrative.executive_view: Contract and private-label cosmetics manufacturing offers recurring programs, meaningful document workflows, and a larger estimated target pool, but the NAICS code also contains own-brand producers. The narrowed lens makes eligibility and customer concentration the central diligence questions.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist formula and specification comparison, batch-record review, label and ingredient checks, deviation triage, planning, forecasting, customer requests, maintenance, and vision inspection. It cannot replace physical blending, line changeovers, material handling, sanitation, sampling, or accountable product release.
narrative.value_capture: Initial labor and error savings accrue under per-unit or program prices, but brands can demand concessions at renewal or rebid. Qualification costs, speed-to-launch, packaging compatibility, formulation knowledge, and service reliability give strong operators some protection.
narrative.firm_availability: The injected 186-firm estimate is sizeable, yet only a fraction are likely contract manufacturers or co-packers under the narrowed lens. Hybrid owned-brand/contract firms, facility registrations, ownership, standalone economics, and customer mix require firm-level verification.
narrative.demand_durability: Consumer personal-care demand is recurrent and recent real spending grew, while outsourcing can help brands manage product variety and regulation. Imports, brand volatility, retailer pressure, and customer insourcing are the main threats to domestic contract volume.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: No public source directly measures the lens's occupation mix, AI labor savings, eligible-firm share, transfers, or contract pass-through. MoCRA increases data and process requirements but also raises the cost of errors; automation must preserve validation, privacy, traceability, and accountable release.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat formulation and packaging programs create durable customer workflows where AI can reduce document, planning, and quality-review effort.
narrative.principal_weakness: The six-digit code mixes brand owners with contract manufacturers, so the truly eligible target pool and its transfer rate are unobserved.

sources.title: NAICS 325620: Toilet Preparation Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the industry as preparing, blending, compounding, and packaging perfumes, shaving and hair preparations, creams, lotions including sunscreens, and other cosmetics.

sources.title: Largest Occupations in Chemical Manufacturing (3255 and 3256 only), May 2024
sources.supports: Reports 18,180 packaging/filling operators and 13,830 mixing/blending operators among the largest occupations in the broader 3255/3256 group.

sources.title: O*NET: Mixing and Blending Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders
sources.supports: Lists weighing, work-order reading, monitoring, mixing, formula following, inspection, recording, sampling, cleaning, maintenance, and testing tasks; 77% report standing continually or almost continually.

sources.title: O*NET: Packaging and Filling Machine Operators and Tenders
sources.supports: Lists labeling, weighing, inspection, malfunction response, line monitoring, packaging, material replenishment, recording, cleaning, and repair tasks; 70% report standing continually or almost continually.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Census BTOS analysis reports overall AI use around 17-20% in Dec. 2025-May 2026 and higher use among firms with at least 100 employees.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing
sources.supports: Identifies predictive maintenance, quality control, and demand forecasting uses and barriers including data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-system integration.

sources.title: Registration and Listing of Cosmetic Product Facilities and Products
sources.supports: States that manufacturers and processors must register facilities and renew biennially, and that FDA may suspend a facility registration when serious adverse-health conditions are met.

sources.title: BEA Table 2.4.6: Real Personal Consumption Expenditures by Type of Product
sources.supports: Reports real US personal-care-products consumption of 180.942 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 and 188.392 billion in 2025.

sources.title: About Business Dynamics Statistics
sources.supports: Explains that BDS measures openings, closings, startups, shutdowns, job flows, firm age, and size, but not qualifying control transfers.
